analytical_frameworks: Cost-curve-driven disruption (Clean Disruption/RethinkX method): when a technology's cost declines on a compounding curve while an incumbent's is flat, crossover triggers rapid non-linear substitution — analysis starts from the cost curves
Convergence framing: disruptions arrive from several technologies maturing simultaneously (batteries + EVs + autonomy), so single-technology analysis systematically mistimes
S-curve adoption over linear forecasts: adoption is slow, then sudden; official linear forecasts under-predict every major substitution
10x-cheaper rule: order-of-magnitude cost improvement creates new markets and business models, not just cheaper versions of old ones
Systems-collapse analysis: incumbents fail as systems (asset values, financing, talent) once substitution passes the tipping point — decline is faster than diffusion models suggest
recurring_themes: Mainstream institutional forecasts (agencies, incumbents) systematically under-predict technology substitution
Disruption economics are decided by physics-driven cost curves, not by incumbent intentions
Stranded assets accumulate quietly before repricing suddenly
The disruption creates new system architectures, not drop-in replacements
known_cautions: Warns that averaging expert opinion produces exactly the linear forecasts that history falsifies
Cautions against reading early-adoption flatness as failure of the S-curve thesis
known_biases: Aggressive-timeline record: publicly dated substitution calls have sometimes been early — velocity conviction can outrun deployment friction
Cost-curve primacy: regulatory, cultural, and integration frictions receive less native weight
Disruption-selection: the method finds crossovers; domains without clean cost curves fit it poorly

sources.title: Clean Disruption of Energy and Transportation
RethinkX sector reports (Rethinking Transportation, Energy, Food, with co-authors)
Public lectures on cost-curve disruption methodology

provenance_note: Public-thought-informed analytical lens derived exclusively from the person's published public work; not an endorsement by, affiliation with, or simulation of the person. Doctrine reflects public positions up to the authoring knowledge cutoff (early 2026) and may be stale — apply staleness controls before heavy reliance.
forecasting_style: Dated, quantitative substitution forecasts derived from compounding cost curves and S-curve adoption — deliberately contrarian to consensus linear projections; historically directionally right with timing optimism as the known error mode.
